title: Grant Secretary Hutch
summary: A desktop secretary hutch with a bank of little drawers, a shelf, and a flip-down writing desk on hinges — the classic organized workspace, built to sit on a base cabinet.
description:
A secretary hutch is that lovely piece of furniture where a flip-down panel becomes a writing desk, backed by cubbies and small drawers. This one sits on a base unit and packs six little drawers, an adjustable or fixed shelf, mitered edge moulding, and the signature hinged desktop. It's detailed casework, but every joint is simple — screws, pocket holes, and finish nails.

The carcass is 1x12 (or plywood ripped to width) with a 1/4" back. Because 1x12s vary in width, the plan tells you to measure yours and adjust — do that, or the drawers you build to fit won't. The back is what squares the whole hutch, so tack it on only after you've confirmed the box is square. The six drawers are just little 1x3 boxes with plywood bottoms; make them to fit their openings.

Two details matter for how it works. The flip-down desk needs hinge supports (folding stays) so it holds level and doesn't slam down when open — a plain hinge alone will let it drop. And critically, when you assemble the piece onto its base, screw the hutch to the base: a hutch with a loaded flip-down desktop can tip forward, so it has to be anchored to the base (and, if you stack a top hutch, screw that down too). Miter the edge moulding at 45° for a clean finished front.

tools.note: Optional; 2" screws or pocket screws both work for the carcass.

materials.name: Low-profile hinges and desk stays
materials.note: For the flip-down desk — stays keep it from dropping.

cutList.note: Measure the gap and cut to fit.

steps.title: Cut the base, sides, and top
steps.body: The base is a 46-1/2" board or plywood cut to size. Cut the sides — 1x12s vary in width, so measure yours and adjust the cuts to match. Add the top with screws and glue since it isn't fully face-framed, and adjust for square.

steps.title: Tack on the back
steps.body: Tack the 1/4" back onto the SQUARE hutch with finish nails and glue (or short screws). The back is what holds the box square, so confirm square first.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body: Make six little drawers — simple 1x3 boxes (finish nails and glue are fine) with 1/4" plywood tacked to the bottom. Build them to fit their openings.

steps.title: Fit the shelf and moulding
steps.body: Insert the drawer sections and measure the gap for the shelf (about 22"), cutting to fit. Make it fixed or drill for shelf pins. Then cut the edge moulding to fit and nail and glue it down, mitering the corners at 45°.

steps.title: Hang the flip-down desk and anchor
steps.body: Attach the flip-down desktop to the desk interior with low-profile hinges, and add hinge supports (stays) so it holds level when open instead of dropping. On assembly, screw the hutch to its base — a loaded flip-down top can tip the hutch forward, so it must be anchored (and screw down any top hutch too). Fill, sand, and finish.

images.alt: Secretary hutch with small drawers and a flip-down writing desk
